summary: A harpsichord suite in B minor by J. S. Bach (BWV 831), published in Clavier-Übung II alongside the Italian Concerto in F major. It exemplifies the French overture form: a slow section with dotted rhythms followed by a fast fugal section. Originally composed in C minor, it was transposed to B minor to complete the cycle of tonalities in the collection. The suite concludes with an 'Echo' movement emphasizing dynamic contrasts. The Goldberg Variations' sixteenth variation is also in French overture form. Jean-Baptiste Lully developed the French overture form in his ballets and operas.

works.name: Overture in the French Style
works.description: A harpsichord suite in B minor (BWV 831) by J. S. Bach, published in Clavier-Übung II alongside the Italian Concerto in F major. Originally composed in C minor, transposed to B minor to complete the collection's cycle of tonalities. Concludes with an 'Echo' movement emphasizing dynamic contrasts. Exemplifies the French overture form (slow dotted-rhythm introduction followed by fast fugal section), developed by Jean-Baptiste Lully in his operas and ballets. Handel's Water Music (composed for King George I's Thames barge trip) also opens with a French overture. The 16th Goldberg Variation is also in this form.

comprehensive_summary:
The Overture in the French Style (BWV 831) is a harpsichord suite in B minor by J. S. Bach, published in the Clavier-Übung II (1735) alongside the Italian Concerto in F major. The pairing was deliberate, contrasting French and Italian musical styles. The work was originally composed in C minor but transposed to B minor to complete the cycle of tonalities in the collection. It concludes with an 'Echo' movement that emphasizes dynamic contrasts.

The work exemplifies the French overture form: a slow introduction characterized by dotted rhythms, followed by a fast fugal or imitative section. This form was developed by Jean-Baptiste Lully and first appeared appended to early operas such as Robert Cambert's Pomone and Lully's Atys; Lully used it in his intermèdes, tragédies lyriques, and comédies-ballets. Telemann wrote over a hundred suites in overture form. Handel's Water Music (composed for a Thames barge trip planned by King George I) also opens with a French overture, as does Les Indes galantes by Rameau. The 16th variation of Bach's Goldberg Variations is also in French overture form.
recursive_suggestions: Italian Concerto (Bach) — always paired with the Overture in the French Style in Clavier-Übung II
Clavier-Übung II — the Bach collection containing both works
